text: In thymectomy, less invasive surgical options have arisen as alternatives to median synotomy, but thymectomy or robotic thymectomy through the unilateral or bilateral approach.
However, the lateral approach causes intercostal nerve injury.

The surgeon performs a single-port thymectomy using specialized devices through an infrasoundary approach, focusing on minimally invasive techniques.

The video displays the instruments used for a single-port thymectomy, arranged and ready for use in the surgical procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the single-port used for thymectomy, highlighting its compatibility with CO₂ insufflation for enhanced visualization during the procedure.
The surgeon uses grasping forceps with the left hand and a vessel sealing device with the right hand during the single-port thymectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned supine with legs spread under general anesthesia, preparing for the single-port thymectomy procedure.

The surgeon makes a three-centimeter transverse incision one centimeter below the xiphoid process, initiating the surgical approach for the thymectomy.
The surgeon performs blind dissection of the posterior surface of the sternum using their finger, manipulating the tissue without direct visual guidance.
The surgeon inserts a single-port device through the subxiphoid incision, preparing for the thymectomy procedure.
CO₂ insufflation is performed at a pressure of eight millimeters of mercury through the subxiphoid incision, creating space for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to detach the thymus from the posterior surface of the sternum, carefully separating the thymus tissue from the surrounding structures.
The insufflated CO₂ displaces the lungs and pericardium, visibly expanding the space behind the sternum during the detachment of the thymus.
The surgeon dissects the mediastinum, opening up the chest cavity bilaterally, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon identifies the bilateral phrenic nerve placements within the chest cavity, ensuring careful navigation around these critical structures during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon dissects the thymus, thymic tumor, and surrounding fatty tissue from the pericardium, ensuring these structures are separated anterior to the phrenic nerves.
The surgeon isolates the brachiocephalic vein, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues within the opened chest cavity.
The surgeon separates the right upper pole of the sinus, revealing the anatomical structures and creating space within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealing device to cut the thymic vein, completing the dissection of the thymus and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon places the resected thymus into a specimen bag within the mediastinum, following its removal from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon removes the thymus, thymic tumor, and surrounding fatty tissue through the sub-xiphoid incision.
A small French drain is inserted through the sub-xiphoid incision, positioned within the surgical field.
We have performed this operation in forty-seven patients.
Ten patients had myasthenia gravis.
The mean operative time was one thirty-nine point eight minutes.
The mean operative blood loss was two point nine grams.
One patient had atrial fibrillation and one patient had phrenic nerve paralysis after operation.
A camera scope is inserted through the subxiphoid incision, providing a clear view of the neck area and post-clinic nerves, similar to the visualization achieved in median sternotomy.
This procedure offers excellent benefits such as no need for sternotomy, no intercostal nerve injury, less pain, and aesthetically favorable.
